skill.name: azure-ai-openai-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance for building .NET applications with Azure OpenAI and the OpenAI SDK. It covers clients, authentication, chat, embeddings, images, audio, structured outputs, tools, RAG, and error handling.
skill.summary: Use Azure OpenAI and OpenAI SDK patterns in .NET applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical heuristic risk from code execution, network, credential, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. AI review found the code execution, weak-crypto, filesystem, reconnaissance, and obfuscation alerts are false positives caused by markdown examples and SDK documentation. The remaining low risk is that examples reference service endpoints and API key environment variables.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer matched markdown backtick fences and inline code as Ruby or shell execution. These lines document package installation and SDK type names; the skill does not include runnable Ruby, scripts, or automatic command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are markdown code fences, inline SDK names, or package commands in documentation. No executable script file, Ruby code, or automated command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Documented Network Endpoints and Reference Links
low_findings.description: The skill contains Azure and Microsoft documentation URLs plus endpoint placeholders. These are expected for SDK guidance and do not show data exfiltration or unauthorized outbound requests by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are service endpoint examples and reference links. There is no evidence of hidden collection, encoded payloads, or transmission to unrelated domains.

low_findings.title: API Key Environment Variable Examples
low_findings.description: The skill shows how sample .NET code reads Azure OpenAI endpoint and API key values from environment variables. This is legitimate SDK usage, but users should prefer identity-based authentication for production systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable access is real in the sample code, including an API key. The context is transparent authentication documentation and it also recommends DefaultAzureCredential for production.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto, Filesystem, and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts map to ordinary SDK documentation text, tool parameter descriptions, and best-practice guidance. The filesystem alert is from a token scope string and does not access hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation strings and best-practice text at these locations. No weak cryptographic API, system reconnaissance command, or hidden-file read is present.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable .NET patterns for Azure OpenAI without searching across SDK docs. This skill provides focused C# examples for clients, authentication, model features, and production usage.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install Azure.AI.OpenAI and related .NET packages.
Explains AzureOpenAIClient setup with API keys and DefaultAzureCredential.
Provides C# patterns for chat, streaming, structured outputs, and reasoning models.
Covers embeddings, image generation, audio transcription, and text-to-speech clients.
Demonstrates Azure AI Search data source setup for retrieval-augmented generation.
Summarizes SDK types, error handling, rate limits, and production best practices.
limitations: It is documentation-only and does not include runnable project files or tests.
Version details may need verification against the latest NuGet release.
Examples require an existing Azure OpenAI resource and model deployments.
Security, quota, and compliance settings must be reviewed for each Azure tenant.

use_cases.title: Prototype chat features
use_cases.description: Use the chat, streaming, and options examples to add Azure OpenAI responses to a .NET application.

use_cases.title: Plan production authentication
use_cases.description: Compare API key setup with DefaultAzureCredential and choose a safer deployment pattern.

use_cases.title: Add multimodal AI workflows
use_cases.description: Use the image, audio, embeddings, and RAG examples to design richer AI features.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an AzureOpenAIClient in a .NET app using DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Add chat streaming
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me adapt the streaming chat example for a C# web app that displays tokens as they arrive.

prompt_templates.title: Design structured output
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a strict structured output plan for a .NET Azure OpenAI feature that returns validated business data.

prompt_templates.title: Review production architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure OpenAI .NET design for authentication, retry handling, token tracking, RAG, and tool call validation.

output_examples.input: User asks for a chat completion setup in C#.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering package installation, AzureOpenAIClient creation, ChatClient usage, and response handling.

output_examples.input: User asks how to use Azure AI Search with Azure OpenAI.
output_examples.output: A RAG-oriented checklist covering search endpoint configuration, index selection, authentication, citations, and validation.

output_examples.input: User asks for production readiness guidance.
output: Handle rate limits and token costs.
Validate tool call arguments before execution.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential for production services and avoid storing API keys in source control.
Reuse SDK clients, set timeouts, and implement retry behavior for rate limits.
Validate structured outputs and tool call arguments before using model-generated data.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Azure OpenAI API keys or endpoint secrets in application code.
Do not create a new client for every request in a high-traffic service.
Do not execute tool calls from the model without argument validation and authorization checks.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you build .NET applications that call Azure OpenAI and OpenAI SDK clients.

faq.question: Does it support Azure authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers API key authentication and DefaultAzureCredential for Microsoft Entra ID.

faq.question: Can I use it for embeddings and RAG?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes embeddings examples and Azure AI Search data source guidance.

faq.question: Does it generate images or audio?
faq.answer: It documents SDK patterns for image generation, audio transcription, and text-to-speech.

faq.question: Is this a runnable sample project?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with snippets and reference patterns.

faq.question: Which AI coding tools can use it?
faq.answer: It is listed for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
